title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: heat map drawn ggplot2::geom_tile() carries three dimensions information: x position, y position, single value mapped fill. plenty one number per cell, forces facet (draw two separate maps) moment want compare two data sources footing. ggtaichi removes limitation replacing cell taichi (yin-yang) diagram. symbol circle split S-curve two interlocking “fish”: yang (light) fish shaded one data source, yin (dark) fish shaded . fish live cell, single geom_taichi() layer encodes four dimensions : x, y, yin, yang. two sources keep color scales legends, can read independently still compared side side. decorative eyes markers – every drop ink plot mapped data.
code: library(ggtaichi) library(ggplot2)

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: worth zooming one cell see anatomy glyph. yang fish (bulb bottom) carries one source; yin fish (bulb top) carries . half filled gradient, lighter darker shade smaller larger value.  yang (red) fish reads 7 yin (grey) fish reads 3; deeper ink, larger number relative rest data.
code: one <- data.frame(x = 1, y = 1, google = 7, twitter = 3)  ggplot(one, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = twitter, yang = google) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi()

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: ggtaichi ships data sets used foundational package ggDoubleHeat. pitts_tg records 30-week COVID-related Google Twitter incidence rates 9 categories Pittsburgh Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA). states_tg larger sibling, repeating measurements across four states, pitts_emojis holds popular weekly emoji per category. See ?pitts_tg, ?states_tg, ?pitts_emojis full descriptions.
code: head(pitts_tg) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   msa         week week_start category          Twitter Google #>   <chr>      <int> <date>     <chr>               <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1 Pittsburgh     1 2020-06-01 Covid              0.965  0.681  #> 2 Pittsburgh     1 2020-06-01 General Virus      0.538  0.0982 #> 3 Pittsburgh     1 2020-06-01 Masks              0.466  0.117  #> 4 Pittsburgh     1 2020-06-01 Sanitizing         0.0561 0.127  #> 5 Pittsburgh     1 2020-06-01 Social Distancing  0.294  0.0386 #> 6 Pittsburgh     1 2020-06-01 Symptoms           0.0457 0.0770

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: two value columns passed yin yang arguments. Everything else – x/y mapping, faceting, titles – plain ggplot2. legend titles default column names supplied (Twitter Google ).  symbol stays round regardless panel’s aspect ratio, need coord_fixed(). shape sized square units, like radius grid::circleGrob().
code: ggplot(pitts_tg, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   theme_taichi() +   ggtitle("Pittsburgh Google & Twitter Incidence Rate (%)")

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: Thirty weeks across nine categories lot ink one panel. goal read individual symbols rather scan overall texture, subset data: fewer cells means taichi drawn larger.
code: pitts_small <- subset(pitts_tg, week <= 6)  ggplot(pitts_small, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   theme_taichi() +   ggtitle("The first six weeks, drawn large")

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: yin defaults grey (luminance) ramp yang red ramp, echoing “ink seal” look classic taichi. choice , useful rule thumb put source want read intensity yin (eye reads darkness quickly) source want read warmth yang.

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: fish gets gradient. yang_colors yin_colors accept color vector (usually hex codes), yang_name / yin_name relabel legends. extra argument forwarded ggplot2::scale_fill_gradientn(), can, example, set common limits legends share scale, pass na.value.
code: ggplot(pitts_small, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(     yin = Twitter,  yin_name = "Twitter (%)",     yin_colors = c("#deebf7", "#3182bd", "#08306b"),     yang = Google, yang_name = "Google (%)",     yang_colors = c("#fee6ce", "#e6550d", "#7f2704")   ) +   theme_taichi()

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: ggplot2 leaves margin around discrete continuous scales, can make taichi grid look like floating. remove_padding() trims ; tell whether axis continuous ("c") discrete ("d").
code: ggplot(pitts_small, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   remove_padding(x = "c", y = "d") +   theme_taichi()

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: geom_taichi() ordinary layer, faceting works box. states_tg data set carries measurements across four states; pairing two weeks keeps every glyph large legible.
code: two_states <- subset(states_tg, state %in% c("New York", "Texas") & week <= 6)  ggplot(two_states, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   facet_wrap(~ state, ncol = 1) +   remove_padding(x = "c", y = "d") +   theme_taichi() +   ggtitle("New York vs Texas, weeks 1-6")

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: theme_taichi() light, -white companion theme bottoms legends, drops panel grid ticks, emphasizes axis labels. normal ggplot2 theme, can override element afterwards, skip entirely bring .
code: ggplot(pitts_small, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   theme_taichi() +   theme(plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white")) +   ggtitle("theme_taichi(), then tweaked")

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: ggtaichi stands shoulders ggDoubleHeat package, pioneered two-source “double” heat map geom_heat_*() family supplies example data used throughout vignette. Please cite alongside ggtaichi: Yu Y, Buskirk T (2025). ggDoubleHeat: Heatmap-Like Visualization Tool. R package version 0.1.3. CRAN: https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=ggDoubleHeat, GitHub: https://github.com/PursuitOfDataScience/ggDoubleHeat

title: Authors and Citation
text: Youzhi Yu. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Yu Y (2026). ggtaichi: Taichi-Diagram Visualization Two Data Sources. R package version 0.1.0, https://pursuitofdatascience.github.io/ggtaichi/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {ggtaichi: Taichi-Diagram Visualization for Two Data Sources},   author = {Youzhi Yu},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://pursuitofdatascience.github.io/ggtaichi/}, }

what: Taichi-Diagram Visualization for Two Data Sources
title: Taichi-Diagram Visualization for Two Data Sources
text: ggtaichi ggplot2 extension compares data two sources single grid taichi (yin-yang) diagrams. regular heat map made geom_tile() encodes three dimensions (x, y position one value); geom_taichi() turns every cell taichi symbol whose two interlocking fish filled two sources , four dimensions expressed one plot.

title: Taichi-Diagram Visualization for Two Data Sources
text: can install development version GitHub :
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("PursuitOfDataScience/ggtaichi")

title: Taichi-Diagram Visualization for Two Data Sources
text: symbol circle split S-curve two interlocking fish. yang (light) fish shaded one source yin (dark) fish , gradient. decorative dots: every drop ink data.
code: library(ggtaichi) library(ggplot2)  one <- data.frame(x = 1, y = 1, google = 7, twitter = 3)  ggplot(one, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = twitter, yang = google) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi()

title: Taichi-Diagram Visualization for Two Data Sources
text: built-pitts_tg dataset holds 30-week COVID-related Google Twitter incidence rates 9 categories Pittsburgh Metropolitan Statistical Area. many weeks symbols shrink, often easier read slice. first six weeks, taichi big enough compare two halves glance.  legend titles default column names supply. Note Covid Masks lean dark (high Twitter) staying pink (moderate Google).
code: pitts_small <- subset(pitts_tg, week <= 6)  ggplot(pitts_small, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   theme_taichi() +   ggtitle("Pittsburgh: Google (yang) vs Twitter (yin), weeks 1-6")

title: Taichi-Diagram Visualization for Two Data Sources
text: fish gets gradient, extra argument passed straight ggplot2::scale_fill_gradientn().
code: ggplot(pitts_small, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(     yin  = Twitter, yin_name  = "Twitter (%)",     yin_colors  = c("#deebf7", "#3182bd", "#08306b"),     yang = Google,  yang_name = "Google (%)",     yang_colors = c("#fee6ce", "#e6550d", "#7f2704")   ) +   theme_taichi()

title: Taichi-Diagram Visualization for Two Data Sources
text: geom_taichi() ordinary layer, faceting just works. states_tg dataset repeats measurements across four states; showing two handful weeks keeps glyphs large legible.  See vignette("ggtaichi") full tour.
code: two_states <- subset(states_tg, state %in% c("New York", "Texas") & week <= 6)  ggplot(two_states, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   facet_wrap(~ state, ncol = 1) +   remove_padding(x = "c", y = "d") +   theme_taichi() +   ggtitle("New York vs Texas, weeks 1-6")

title: Taichi-Diagram Visualization for Two Data Sources
text: ggtaichi built top , spiritual sibling , ggDoubleHeat package, introduced idea folding two data sources single reformed heat map geom_heat_*() family. ggtaichi reuses two-scale design (example data) re-imagines per-cell glyph taichi diagram. ggDoubleHeat foundational layer package cited use ggtaichi: Yu Y, Buskirk T (2025). ggDoubleHeat: Heatmap-Like Visualization Tool. R package version 0.1.3. CRAN: https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=ggDoubleHeat, GitHub: https://github.com/PursuitOfDataScience/ggDoubleHeat
code: @Manual{,   title  = {ggDoubleHeat: A Heatmap-Like Visualization Tool},   author = {Youzhi Yu and Trent Buskirk},   year   = {2025},   note   = {R package version 0.1.3.             GitHub: https://github.com/PursuitOfDataScience/ggDoubleHeat},   url    = {https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=ggDoubleHeat}, }

title: Taichi — geom_taichi
text: taichi geom turns cell heatmap-like grid taichi (yin-yang) diagram. two interlocking "fish" diagram use luminance show values two data sources plot, four dimensions data can expressed : x y position every taichi symbol plus yin yang values fill two halves.

title: Taichi — geom_taichi
code: geom_taichi(   yin,   yin_name = NULL,   yin_colors = c("gray100", "gray85", "gray50", "gray35", "gray0"),   yang,   yang_name = NULL,   yang_colors = c("#FED7D8", "#FE8C91", "#F5636B", "#E72D3F", "#C20824"),   ... )

title: Taichi — geom_taichi
text: yin column name yin (dark) fish taichi symbol. yin_name label name (quotes) legend yin rendering. Default NULL. yin_colors color vector, usually hex codes. yang column name yang (light) fish taichi symbol. yang_name label name (quotes) legend yang rendering. Default NULL. yang_colors color vector, usually hex codes. ... ... accepts arguments scale_fill_gradientn() .

title: Taichi — geom_taichi
text: taichi diagram comparing two data sources.

title: Taichi — geom_taichi
code: # taichi with categorical variables only  library(ggplot2)  data <- data.frame(x = rep(c("a", "b", "c"), 3),                    y = rep(c("d", "e", "f"), 3),                    yin_values = rep(c(1,5,7),3),                    yang_values = rep(c(2,3,4),3))  ggplot(data, aes(x,y)) + geom_taichi(yin = yin_values,             yang = yang_values)    # taichi with numeric variables only  data <- data.frame(x = rep(c(1, 2, 3), 3),                    y = rep(c(1, 2, 3), 3),                    yin_values = rep(c(1,5,7),3),                    yang_values = rep(c(2,3,4),3))  ggplot(data, aes(x,y)) + geom_taichi(yin = yin_values,             yang = yang_values)    # taichi with a mixture of numeric and categorical variables  data <- data.frame(x = rep(c("a", "b", "c"), 3),                    y = rep(c(1, 2, 3), 3),                    yin_values = rep(c(1,5,7),3),                    yang_values = rep(c(2,3,4),3))  ggplot(data, aes(x,y)) + geom_taichi(yin = yin_values,             yang = yang_values)

what: ggtaichi: Taichi diagrams for two data sources — ggtaichi-package
title: ggtaichi: Taichi diagrams for two data sources — ggtaichi-package
text: ggtaichi, ggplot2 extension, visualizes data two different sources single grid taichi (yin-yang) diagrams. Instead facetting heatmap data source, two sources combined one plot, every cell becomes taichi symbol whose two fish filled two sources via luminance. Prior using package, users load ggplot2.

title: ggtaichi: Taichi diagrams for two data sources — ggtaichi-package
text: main workhorse geom_taichi(), turns every (x, y) cell taichi diagram, much like geom_tile() draws regular heatmap. supported theme_taichi() remove_padding() styling. Users reference documentation run examples help files trying understand argument means visually.

title: ggtaichi: Taichi diagrams for two data sources — ggtaichi-package
text: Maintainer: Youzhi Yu yuyouzhi666@icloud.com

One quick note columns data set: week_start column present data set illustration purposes, reminding users week column . words, participate visualization.

code: pitts_tg

title: Pittsburgh COVID-related Google & Twitter incidence rates — pitts_tg
text: data frame 270 rows 6 columns: msa Metropolitan statistical area (Pittsburgh ). week week 1 week 30. week_start Monday date week started. category 9 Covid-related categories total. Twitter weekly tweets percentage (%) MSA falling   category. Google weekly Google search percentage (%) MSA falling   category.

title: Pittsburgh COVID-related Google & Twitter incidence rates — pitts_tg
text: Just like states_tg, Google processed Google Health API, Twitter Meltwater, Twitter vendor. data sources processed author package.

title: Remove ggplot2 default padding — remove_padding
text: default ggplot2 plots give certain amount padding continuous discrete variables. Due padding, makes plots generated `geom_taichi()` look like something missing. Depends users' preference, can remove "empty space" using function. thing users need figure whether `x` `y` scales continuous discrete.

title: Remove ggplot2 default padding — remove_padding
code: remove_padding(x = "c", y = "d", ...)

title: Remove ggplot2 default padding — remove_padding
text: x x-axis scale, continuous scale, input "c"; discrete, "d". y y-axis scale, continuous scale, input "c"; discrete, "d". ... ...

title: Remove ggplot2 default padding — remove_padding
text: remove_padding

title: Plot Themes — theme_taichi
text: light theme tuned taichi grid: bottoms legends, drops panel grid axis ticks, gives canvas soft -white background reminiscent rice paper.

title: Plot Themes — theme_taichi
code: theme_taichi(   base_size = 11,   base_family = "",   base_line_size = base_size/22,   base_rect_size = base_size/22 )

title: Plot Themes — theme_taichi
text: base_size base font size base_family base font family base_line_size base size line elements base_rect_size base size rect elements

title: Plot Themes — theme_taichi
text: Adding taichi theme plots generated using ggtaichi package.

title: ggtaichi 0.1.0
text: Initial version. geom_taichi() turns cell grid taichi (yin-yang) diagram, filling two fish values two data sources. Added theme_taichi() remove_padding() helpers. Bundled pitts_tg, states_tg, pitts_emojis data sets.
